speaker: interviewer
text: --to tell me about a trick that was being played.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler had, you know, some of our young boys working for him. And one of them was a wonderful-- well, he could make false papers. He could falsify paper. So among other-- all kinds of papers, he would falsify orders for food, for equipment. And he-- they had the pads, and you have to make the stamps and everything. Well, one day they made orders for SS [INAUDIBLE] Leopold, and that he had to go back to Berlin for his-- Hitler wanted to give him some orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left. And he left just when the war was ending, so that when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so once he would go to Berlin, there was no way of him to come back. We-- I guess Oskar Schindler knew that. So when the war was ending, and when Oskar Schindler called us to the factory to announce that the war had-- was going to end at midnight-- and we listened to the broadcast from-- the BBC broadcast by Winston Churchill-- when he turns to the guards and told them, you can either become war criminals or you can return home to your families, there was nobody to tell them not to.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left. Because who knows. If, you know, Leopold would still be there, he would probably want to kill us. Because the Germans were killing the Jews till the very last moment. As the Allies were coming through the front gate, they were killing the Jews in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were things different for you at Brunnlitz than they had been at Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was different because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had less, you know. We had much less food, but we still had-- we still were together with the same people. And we still didn't starve, and we still didn't freeze. Because the last seven months of the war, when we were under Oskar Schindler's care and under Mrs. Emilie Schindler's care, well, we were protected. Hundreds and hundreds, thousands of all the Jewish people, all my family, all my friends, they were sent on death marches [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they-- 90% of them died. They froze to death. They died of starvation. They died by the bullets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And young women, like my aunt-- my youngest aunt, my Aunt [INAUDIBLE], my mother's youngest sister-- was sent to a camp in Stutthof, which was on the Baltic Sea. A few days before the war ended, the Germans put them in the lifeboats, set them free in the ocean, and machine gunned the lifeboats. And 2,000 women drowned, just days before they would-- would have been liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything is documented. The Germans took such records. They kept such-- everything in such detail. And that-- and then there were some survivors who did not go on that. They survived in the camp. Everything, really, that we know is because of the German records, because there are very few survivors. And Germans themselves were so proud of what they were doing and felt they were doing such excellent job that they kept all the records. To this day, you can find out. My cousin just found, recently, one-- one of my uncles, my father's brother, how he died in Flossenburg by a injection of gasoline two days before the war ended. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And so we on Schindler's List are so unbelievably lucky because, if it was not for Oskar Schindler, I would never be here. I would never have survived. I wouldn't have had a chance to get married and have children and grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like my cousins that were, you know, just as smart as me and just as cute me-- more, but were not given a chance-- were not given a chance to survive. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at midnight the night the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brunnlitz? By midnight, he had to leave. The war was over, and he would be a war criminal. So he change-- he and Mrs. Schindler changed in prisoners' uniforms, even though they didn't look like prisoners because they had hair. And he opened the warehouses. He had a warehouse where he had a lot of textiles, and gave orders to the-- he-- to the young boys, our young Jewish men. He had made contact with the Czech underground, and they-- they armed our boys. He was-- Oskar Schindler was going to leave. He gave orders that every one of us was going to get, like, three yards of fabric and a bottle of whiskey and a pair of slippers. That was going to be our money. That's what was in the warehouse. And the boys were going to guard us with the guns, because the German army was retreating and there was still danger somebody would come in and kill us. And we gave him the ring. They made a ring for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they make the ring out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of gold that was in somebody's tooth. There were a few men that still had gold teeth. See, if they went through Auschwitz, they would not have had gold teeth, but the men went to Gross-Rosen. And they-- some of them still had the teeth, so they took the gold out and made the ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the men go to Gross-Rosen before they came to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The men went to Gross-Rosen. And-- but the men were held in Gross-Rosen like for 24 hours, and then they were let go, unlike the women. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long were you in Auschwitz altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks. Three weeks. And then, we all went and kissed-- with, you know, the whole line of people went by and kissed his hand, Oskar's hand and Mrs. Schindler's hand. And we all cried. I cried. I felt like my father was being taken away again. Again here, I was-- supposedly we were going to be liberated, but we were afraid. And then about 12 young men, again, went with Oskar and Emilie Schindler. We all signed an affidavit that he, indeed, saved us, because he knew that we were going to be liberated by the Russians. And he had to give himself up to the Americans, because the Russian would shoot him. So he-- he said goodbye, and he left. They had some gold. They had some diamonds. Whatever they had, they put in the doors between the car, car doors. And they left. They traveled. They gave-- so that he could give himself up to the American. And we were waiting. And we were liberated by one Russian soldier that came on a horse. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did that happen? About, probably March-- May 10. We waited a few days-- May 10, even May 11 before anybody came for us. And before we were liberated, there was a group of German Wehrmacht, German soldiers, that were going in a truck and stopped by the gate of the camp and said, what is this? What is this? And the guards told them, the people here are dying of typhoid and diphtheria. So they didn't want to go in. They were afraid. But they asked for petrol. They didn't have enough gas to go to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get gas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They gave him gas just to get rid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were they guys, Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, oh, yeah. The Jewish boys. And when the Russian soldier came, I remember he-- he was short. He stood on a table and told them, you are now being liberated by the victorious Russia-- Russian army. And we talked to him and he said he was Jewish. He came from Krakow. He said, when you go back, you won't find anybody left, and you also won't find anybody happy to see you. And that's what happened. We were-- they arranged-- the Russian arranged transport for us to go back. Some people left on their own. Some people just left. They walked. They-- they borrowed a horse or something. They tried to get a train. But we waited. There were a lot of people that waited. And we got to Krakow at night. Probably was beginning of June. And we went-- they-- they took-- since it was late, so they made arrangements for us to go to sleep in one of the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there? How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there may be-- maybe 150 people that took on the train. And this-- the school was right across the street from a-- from an apartment where my aunt and uncle and three cousins lived before the war. And in the morning, we went to our street, my mother and I. And of course, we found nobody. The superintendent of my grandparents' apartment said nobody from our family had come back to look for us. Then we went to the Jewish office of-- for the-- you know, for the refugees, for the survivors, where the walls were plastered with people looking for somebody. Nobody was looking for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother's brothers and sisters, their wives, their children, and their-- her parents did not come back. Of course, my father did not come back. My aunt had died. Six of my father's brothers and their wives and children were murdered. My grandfather survived because he was with us on Schindler's List.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after a few weeks, my father's youngest sister-- only sister-- came. She survived in Bergen-Belsen. And I had five girl cousins who survived on my father's side. Four cousins, four sisters, and survived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to-- on a death march from Auschwitz. And they ran away. They hid the last-- like a day before, or the-- the morning before, the-- the Americans came because they said they can't walk another-- another second. And they hid in a hole, someplace, in a barn. Five of them, four-- four sisters and a friend. And they weren't there more than-- than maybe six hours when the Americans came. And then I had two cousins, two boy cousins, who survived. 11 of my boy cousins were murdered. And, you know, most everybody I knew, they were orphans. Had no-- nobody left. And I had my mother, my grandfather. I had an aunt and an uncle and some cousins. Two of my father's youngest brothers who escaped Russia came back after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew we couldn't stay in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay very long. I think maybe we stayed there four or five weeks. My mother got a job right away. I got a promise of a job that, in September, when school starts, I would be a kindergarten teacher, a nursery school teacher, also through a friend of my father. But then we had-- I had a friend whom I knew came from Austria. And he came and said to my mother and me that a lot of people are in Austria. And there-- there's a displaced per-- persons' camp in Austria, and that we would, you know, try to immigrate. And we didn't-- it was so unbearable in Poland, I couldn't live there. You know, we had nothing. We rented a room someplace. And the Poles were still killing the Jews after the war. They still had pogrom. I have a friend who survived the war, went to Kielce to look for his parents, and was killed by the Polish people there because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said, maybe he was going to look for some of his furniture or some of the things that he-- they left before the war. Also, the Polish Free Army used to stop the trains and look-- search for the trains for returning survivors and would direct them off the train and kill them. So we left. We left and, you know, lived in Linz. That's where I met my husband, we got married and waited to immigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went with your mother and your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather went to Germany with my aunt and uncle. My mother remarried. My mother met-- in Austria, she met a friend from before the war who lost his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the displaced persons camp, she met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She lo-- he lost his wife and a son. And his younger son was a friend of mine. And I found out he was alive, and I told the father. So the father came to us and found my mother. And they got married. They were married for 20 years and came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: At-- tell me how you met your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you meet Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met Mark in-- in Austria, in Linz when I came after the war. And I was with a group of girls, and he was with a group of guys. And we met and talked together. And then he started to come and court me. And of course, we get married very young because my mother wanted to go back to Poland to get married. My stepfather went back to Poland to be-- His son was in Poland. He was sick. He was in a sanitarium. He had tuberculosis of the hip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a-- I worked as a clerk in the United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation office. I was an interpreter. And I typed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and some work. And I got married because I didn't want to go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wedding like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got married. My-- Mark had a cousin came, and he had a little apartment, you know, in this-- we had-- we were living in buildings that were vacated by the German army. But again, everybody had a room. So we had a small room. And my cousin had a smaller room, and he only had a two-room apartment. So in his kitchen, we got married. Had four people who were standing with the chuppa, and I had just 10 people. Enough for a-- enough for the minyan. And it was a very small wedding. I got two wedding gifts. I got a pocketbook-- a leather pocketbook-- from Mark's cousin, and a tablecloth from my girlfriend's parents. I had a girlfriend who survived with her mother, and after the war, she found her father and a brother who survived. So there were a few miracles. And of course, my-- my girlfriend, Sonia, and her sister survived together. So we were all together in the displaced person camp in Linz. And we went to school. Well, actually, we didn't go to school. We were tutored privately because we had so much to catch up. We were so far behind. And we had so much to learn, so we were privately tutored.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came to the United States in 19-- November 19th, 1948. From 1945 to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother stayed in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed in Poland. And then she came to Austria. And it was 10 years before my mother could come, because they were still-- even though she had a daughter here. I think that she had to wait because we had to become citizen. Once we were citizen, we could send for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather came to the United States with my aunt and uncle. And he lived to see a lot of our-- you know, a lot of our children being born. And he died in 1956. He was, I think, 87 years old. He died right after Yom Kippur. He fell down in the temple. And, you know, once-- once you're that old, you fall down. He got pneumonia and he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died very young. My mother died 27 years ago. She was almost 64 years old. She died of aneurysm. She was very young. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and Mark do when you came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the very beginning, Mark worked in a leather factory. And I worked in a sho-- first I worked in a factory where I was testing bulbs, test tubes. And there were a lot of Polish people in that factory, and we were not comfortable. I worked in there with my girlfriend, Blanca, and we were very uncomfortable. There was a lot-- I could feel the antisemitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we got a job in a shoe factory. And it was not what I dreamt of during the war, but we wanted very much to-- to work and make money and build a life. So I worked in that factory till I was pregnant, and then I quit. And then I just stayed home till my children were born. How did you get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My girlfriend's uncle in Peabody, Uncle Harry White, who didn't know us, who was not a rich man, just the opposite. He sent papers when Sonia and Blanca and Norbert came to the United States and told him that we had-- they had friends who didn't really have anybody. He sent us papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to the United States, we came to Peabody. We came on the first ship with displaced persons to Boston. All the other boats before came to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we came to Boston, and they had an apartment waiting for us, and a job for Mark in a leather factory together with Norbert. And then Blanca and I work in a-- went to the shoe factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- our first one, our oldest one, was born in October 20th, 1950, had a birthday on Sunday. And then, 14 months later, we had Marilyn. And then four and 1/2 years later, we had Debbie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and they're married and-- And they all-- they are married, and of course we have six grandchildren. We have five grandsons and one granddaughter. Would you like to tell me about Anita?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Anita got cancer 10 years ago. And she had breast cancer. But when it was discovered, it was too late. It never really showed in her breast. It-- she never had a lump. But it settled in her bones. And so when she was diagnosed, there was already no hope for her. But she fought-- she fought for three and 1/2 years. She was on radiation and she was on chemo. When she was on chemo, she was so sick. Her little girl was just two years old. Just two years old and three months when-- when Anita was diagnosed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Anita suffered a lot. She had two femoral bone replacements because the cancer made her bones so brittle, like Swiss cheese, the doctor said. And they had to operate because they said, if the bone will break, she would be in such pain that no amount of morphine would help. So she died six and 1/2 years ago. She was 39 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bert. Bert got remarried, married a-- a wonderful girl. Her name is Terri. And we're very close. We're very close. We see them a lot. They come here. We're going-- in two weeks, I'm going to babysit. I sis-- babysit for Amy when they go away. So this way I have Amy. I'm able to spend time with Amy, and they're able to go away and not feel too pressured. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So Amy, of course, is a wonderful little girl. She's 12 and 1/2. She will be bat mitzvah next May. And she's a princess amongst the boys. We have a-- a 20-year-old grandson who is a freshman at the University of Wyoming. And we have an 18-year-old-- no, excuse me, the 20-year-old is a junior. And the 18-year-old, Adam, he's a freshman at the State University of Utah. And whose children are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are Marilyn's children. And then Debbie's little boys are Jason, who is nine, and David, who is six. And of course, they are adorable and they live right around the corner. And then, Marilyn divorced and remarried. And she has a little boy who is three years old. He will be three years old in November 9th. Cute.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have you been doing with-- with yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am, of course, connected with Facing History. I have been with them since 1981. And I go to-- I lecture, tell-- talk about the Holocaust in schools, middle school, high school, colleges, all kinds of groups of adult education, churches, and temple. Because I feel, just like the sur-- all the survivors feel very strongly that we must tell our story again and again and again, so that it will never be forgotten. So it cannot happen again. And I feel very strongly, when I speak in schools, to make a point that every one of us can make a difference. That Oskar Schindler and Emilie Schindler are proof that one person can make a difference. That you have to be involved. That you cannot be a bystander. That you cannot really sit by and listen to somebody call somebody else racial slurs. You have to stand up. When you see somebody painting a swastika on a church or temple or wherever, you really have to stand up and tell on that person. You cannot cover it. You-- it has to be investigated. Who-- the perpetrator has to be punished. They have to be taught that hatred and prejudice has never brought us any happiness. Has never brought us any riches. And that I think that the only hope for mankind is if we really learn how to live with each other. Otherwise, I see no future for my grandchildren and those yet to come. And I try to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] explain to the people who listen to me what hatred, what prejudice, has done, when the whole world-- whole world stood by and did nothing and allowed innocent people to be slaughtered. Nobody said anything. Everybody said there was nothing I could do. And that's a lie, because there is always something you can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this why you decided to give your testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I feel the survivors are all getting older. And every day, there are fewer of us left. Every day I hear of somebody who has passed away. In a few more years, there'll be none of us left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to, as long as we-- we have the chance, the opportunity, the strength to speak, we speak for-- for all the six millions of our brothers and sisters and cousins and all those who have no-- no voices. We are the voices for those who were murdered. OK. Let's stop now. We'll finish up on the next tape.
